title: Exception Handling
description: Understand Java's exception hierarchy, master try/catch/finally control flow, and learn to write robust programs with custom exceptions — exactly as tested in CSE110 exam predict-output and error-identification questions.

sections.content: ⚠️ Why Exception Handling Dominates CSE110 Exams

Exception handling is one of the highest-yield topics in CSE110 because it introduces **non-linear control flow**: a `throw` statement can jump over multiple lines, a `finally` block always runs (even when you think it shouldn't), and catching the wrong type silently lets errors propagate. Exam questions exploit every one of these surprises. This chapter traces every execution path so you are never surprised.

Java exceptions are objects in an inheritance hierarchy. At the top is `Throwable`. Its two children are `Error` (JVM failures like `OutOfMemoryError` — you don't catch these) and `Exception` (program-level problems — you do catch these). `Exception` divides into **checked exceptions** (must be declared or caught) and **unchecked exceptions** (`RuntimeException` and its children — optional to catch).

sections.label: Most Common Exam Mistake
sections.content: Confusing checked vs unchecked exceptions. `NullPointerException` and `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` are **unchecked** (extend `RuntimeException`) — you do NOT need to declare them with `throws`. `IOException` is **checked** — you MUST either catch it or declare `throws IOException`. Exam questions test this distinction directly.
🔧 try / catch / finally: Syntax and Control Flow

The `try` block contains code that might throw. Each `catch` block handles a specific exception type. The `finally` block runs **always** — whether an exception was thrown, caught, or not thrown at all. This is the most exam-critical property of `finally`.

sections.title: Basic try/catch/finally Structure

sections.content: 🔗 Multiple catch Blocks and catch Order

You can chain multiple `catch` blocks. Java checks them **top to bottom** and executes only the **first matching** catch. This means: always place more-specific (child) exception types before more-general (parent) types. Placing a parent type first makes the child type's catch block **unreachable** — a compile error.

sections.title: Multiple catch — Correct Ordering

sections.label: Unreachable catch Block
sections.content: If you write `catch (Exception e)` before `catch (ArithmeticException e)`, the compiler flags a **compile-time error**: `exception ArithmeticException has already been caught`. Since `ArithmeticException` extends `RuntimeException` which extends `Exception`, the general handler catches everything — making the specific handler unreachable.

The `throws` keyword in a method signature declares that the method **may throw** a checked exception, delegating handling to the caller. The `throw` keyword (no `s`) **actually throws** an exception object. These are two different things — a common source of exam confusion.

rows: Statement that actually throws an exception instance
Method signature declaration for checked exceptions
Block that handles a specific exception type
Block that always executes regardless of exception

sections.title: throw vs throws — Method Chain

sections.content: 🔄 finally and return: The Tricky Interaction

The `finally` block runs even when a `return` statement is inside `try` or `catch`. More surprisingly: if `finally` itself contains a `return`, it **overrides** any `return` in `try` or `catch`. This is a notorious exam trap.

sections.title: finally Overrides return — Classic Exam Trap

sections.content: You can create your own exception classes by extending `Exception` (checked) or `RuntimeException` (unchecked). Custom exceptions improve readability and allow domain-specific error types. CSE110 exams may ask you to trace programs that throw and catch custom exceptions.

sections.title: Custom Exception — InsufficientFundsException
Exercise 1: Classic finally Execution Trace
sections.answer:
start
try
catch: oops
finally
end
sections.explanation:
Execution trace line by line:
1. `"start"` prints.
2. Enter try block, `"try"` prints.
3. `if (true)` is always true → `throw new RuntimeException("oops")` executes. The remaining try-block line `"after throw"` is **skipped**.
4. RuntimeException matches `catch (RuntimeException e)` → prints `"catch: oops"` (getMessage() returns `"oops"`).
5. `finally` block always executes → prints `"finally"`.
6. Execution continues after try-catch-finally → prints `"end"`.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Uncaught Exception and finally
sections.answer:
A
C
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
sections.explanation:
1. Prints `"A"`.
2. `arr[5] = 10` on a size-3 array throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. `"B"` is skipped.
3. The catch block handles `NullPointerException` — `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` is a **different type** and does **not match**. The catch block is skipped.
4. `finally` still runs → prints `"C"`.
5. Since the exception was never caught, it propagates up. `main` has no outer handler, so the JVM prints the exception and terminates. `"D"` is **never reached**.

sections.title: Exercise 3: finally with return — What Gets Returned?
sections.answer:
finally runs
try
finally runs
catch
sections.explanation:
**Call 1: `test(false)`**: No exception thrown → `return "try"` is staged. Before returning, `finally` runs → prints `"finally runs"`. Then the staged return value `"try"` is returned. Main prints `"try"`.
**Call 2: `test(true)`**: `throw new Exception()` → jumps to catch → `return "catch"` is staged. Before returning, `finally` runs → prints `"finally runs"`. Then `"catch"` is returned. Main prints `"catch"`.
**Key rule**: `finally` always runs before the method returns, even if `try` or `catch` contains a `return`. The staged return value is preserved unless `finally` itself has a `return`.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Wrong Exception Catch Order (Unreachable catch)
sections.error: Line 8 (`catch (NullPointerException e)`): compile-time error — `NullPointerException` has already been caught by the earlier `catch (Exception e)` block. Since `NullPointerException` extends `RuntimeException` which extends `Exception`, the first catch handles all exceptions including NPE, making the second catch block unreachable.
sections.explanation: This is a **compile-time error**: `exception NullPointerException has already been caught`. The Java compiler enforces that more-specific exception types must appear before more-general ones in a catch chain. `Exception` is the parent of all checked and unchecked exceptions — placing it first makes all subsequent catch blocks unreachable. The fix is to place the most-specific types first and the most-general type last.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Missing throws Declaration for Checked Exception
sections.error: Line 5 (`new FileReader(path)`): compile-time error — `FileNotFoundException` (a checked exception that extends `IOException`) is thrown by `FileReader`'s constructor. Since `readFile` neither catches this exception nor declares `throws FileNotFoundException` (or `throws IOException`), the compiler rejects the code.
sections.explanation: This is a **compile-time error**. `FileReader(String)` throws `FileNotFoundException`, which is a **checked exception**. Java requires that every checked exception either be caught in a `try-catch` block or declared in the method's `throws` clause. Unlike `RuntimeException` (unchecked), checked exceptions cannot be silently ignored — the compiler enforces handling. There are two valid fixes: (1) add `throws IOException` to the method signature and handle it in the caller, or (2) wrap the `FileReader` constructor call in a `try-catch` inside `readFile`.

sections.title: Exercise 6: Write a Safe Integer Parser
sections.approach: Use `Integer.parseInt(s)` inside a try block — it throws `NumberFormatException` (an unchecked exception) if the string is not a valid integer. Handle null separately with a null check before parsing (calling `Integer.parseInt(null)` throws NumberFormatException but the message is unclear, so check null first). Return the parsed value on success, -1 on NumberFormatException, 0 on null.
sections.explanation:
**Why this pattern works**: `Integer.parseInt` throws `NumberFormatException` — an unchecked `RuntimeException` — for any non-integer string. Wrapping it in `try-catch` makes the method robust. The null check comes first because `Integer.parseInt(null)` would throw a `NumberFormatException` with a confusing message; explicit null handling gives a clearer error.
**Key learning**: `NumberFormatException` is unchecked, so no `throws` declaration is needed in the method signature. The return values (-1, 0, valid integer) form a simple contract that callers can check.
**Output verification**: `"42"` → Parsed: 42. `"-7"` → Parsed: -7. `"abc"` → Invalid integer: abc. `null` → Null input received. `"12.5"` → Invalid integer: 12.5 (the decimal point makes it not an integer). ✓

items: `Exception` branches into **checked** (must catch or declare) and **unchecked** (`RuntimeException` subclasses — optional).
**`throw`** (no s) is a statement that throws an exception object: `throw new ExType("msg")`.
**`throws`** (with s) in a method signature declares the method may propagate a checked exception.
`finally` **always executes** — even if no exception, even if there's a `return` in try or catch.
If `finally` contains a `return`, it **overrides** any `return` in `try` or `catch`.
Multiple `catch` blocks are checked **top-to-bottom** — place specific types before general types or you get a compile error.
Custom exceptions: extend `Exception` for checked, `RuntimeException` for unchecked. Call `super(message)` in the constructor.
An uncaught exception propagates up the call stack; if unhandled in `main`, the JVM prints the stack trace and terminates.
